front: Explain the OAuth 2.0 flows: authorization code, client credentials, implicit, and device. When do you use each?
back: Authorization code: user-facing apps (web, mobile). User redirected to auth server, gets code, server exchanges code for tokens. Requires client_secret on server side or PKCE for public clients. Most secure for user auth. Client credentials: machine-to-machine (M2M). No user involved. Client ID + secret exchanged directly for access token. Use for: service accounts, CI/CD pipelines, microservice-to-microservice auth. Implicit: deprecated. Was for SPAs (no backend), returned tokens in URL fragment — vulnerable to token leakage. Replaced by auth code + PKCE. Device: for devices with limited input (Smart TV, CLI). Device polls for token while user authorizes on another device.
senior_nuance: Authorization code with PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) is the current standard for SPAs and mobile apps. PKCE generates a code_verifier (random string) + code_challenge (SHA256 of verifier), sends challenge with auth request, sends verifier at token exchange — proves the same client that initiated the flow is completing it. This prevents authorization code interception attacks. For CLI tools: device flow or auth code + PKCE with localhost redirect. Never use implicit flow — it's removed from OAuth 2.1 draft.
quote_to_say: Authorization code + PKCE is the only flow I use for user-facing apps — implicit flow is retired for good reason.

front: What is OpenID Connect (OIDC) and how does it extend OAuth 2.0? What is the ID token?
back: OAuth 2.0 handles authorization (access tokens for API access). OIDC adds authentication (identity layer) on top of OAuth 2.0. OIDC adds: ID token (JWT containing user identity claims: sub, email, name, iss, aud, exp), UserInfo endpoint (/userinfo) for additional claims, discovery endpoint (/.well-known/openid-configuration) for automatic client configuration. ID token: JWT signed by the identity provider (IdP). Client verifies signature, reads claims. Access token: opaque to client, used to call APIs. Scopes: openid (required), profile, email, address.
senior_nuance: ID token is for the client (who is the user?); access token is for the resource server (what can they do?). Never use the ID token to call APIs — it's not designed for resource access. OIDC flows match OAuth 2.0 flows + openid scope. Back-channel logout: IdP notifies all apps when user logs out (push-based, more reliable than front-channel redirect-based logout). For microservices: validate access token at each service (using JWKS endpoint to verify JWT signature) — don't proxy auth through a gateway for every call.
quote_to_say: ID token = who you are, access token = what you can do — I never use them interchangeably.

front: What are the common JWT security pitfalls? How do you implement JWT correctly?
back: Pitfalls: (1) alg:none attack — some libraries accept 'none' algorithm (unsigned JWT); always validate algorithm explicitly. (2) RS256 vs HS256 confusion — if server accepts both, attacker uses public key as HS256 secret to forge tokens; pin algorithm. (3) No expiration (exp) — tokens are valid forever if stolen; always set short exp (15-60min). (4) Storing JWTs in localStorage — accessible to XSS; use httpOnly cookies or in-memory. (5) Not validating iss (issuer) and aud (audience) claims — tokens from other services are accepted. (6) No key rotation — compromised signing key invalidates all tokens.
senior_nuance: JWT revocation is hard — JWTs are stateless by design. Solutions: short expiry + refresh tokens, token blocklist (Redis SET of invalidated JTI claims), or just use opaque tokens with server-side session (simpler). For logout: invalidating a JWT requires a blocklist. Rotating keys: use JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) endpoint — clients fetch public keys by kid (key ID). Rotate keys by adding new key to JWKS, signing new tokens with it, keeping old key for existing tokens' lifetime. JWT size: large claims bloat every request header — use opaque tokens + UserInfo endpoint for rich claims.
quote_to_say: JWTs are not sessions — you cannot revoke them without additional infrastructure. I pick token lifetime based on my revocation tolerance.

front: Compare session-based auth vs token-based (JWT) auth. What are the scalability and security tradeoffs?
back: Session-based: server stores session in Redis/DB, client gets session ID in cookie (httpOnly, Secure, SameSite). Server is stateful — every request hits session store. Revocation is instant (delete session). Scales horizontally with shared Redis. Simpler to implement correctly. Token-based (JWT): server is stateless — token contains claims, validated with public key. No session store needed. Scales without shared state. Revocation requires blocklist (loses stateless benefit). Good for: distributed microservices, third-party API access, mobile clients that can't use cookies.
senior_nuance: Cookies vs Authorization header: cookies have CSRF risk (mitigated by SameSite=Strict/Lax) but are automatically sent by browser and support httpOnly (XSS protection). Authorization: Bearer header requires explicit JavaScript (XSS can steal it from localStorage). For web apps: sessions in httpOnly cookies win on security. For SPAs calling APIs from different origins: CORS + Authorization header with short-lived JWTs + refresh token rotation. Refresh token rotation: issue new refresh token on each use, invalidate old — detects token theft (simultaneous use of old and new token is suspicious).
quote_to_say: Sessions in httpOnly cookies are more secure for web apps — JWTs shine for stateless microservices and third-party API access.

front: How does refresh token rotation work? What makes it secure and what is the security model?
back: Short-lived access tokens (15-60 min) + long-lived refresh token (days/weeks). Refresh flow: client sends expired access token + refresh token -> server validates refresh token, issues new access token + new refresh token, invalidates old refresh token. Rotation: every refresh issues a new refresh token — old is single-use. Security: if refresh token is stolen and used, the server detects simultaneous use of old + new token (old is already invalidated but attacker uses it -> mismatch -> family revocation: invalidate all tokens in the family). Refresh tokens stored: server-side (opaque, DB/Redis) or as signed JWTs.
senior_nuance: Refresh token family: a logical chain of refresh tokens for a session. On theft detection (old token reuse), revoke the entire family — logs out all sessions including legitimate ones (conservative but safe). Balance: revoke only the device's family vs all families. Absolute expiry: refresh tokens should have a maximum lifetime regardless of activity (e.g., 90 days) — prevents indefinite access if token is silently stolen. Silent refresh: SPA refreshes access token in background before expiry using refresh token stored in httpOnly cookie — keeps UX seamless.
quote_to_say: Refresh token rotation gives me a way to detect theft — without it, a stolen refresh token grants indefinite access.

front: Explain PKCE. What attack does it prevent and why is it required for public clients?
back: PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange, RFC 7636): extension to OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow for public clients (those that cannot securely store a client_secret — SPAs, mobile apps, CLIs). Steps: (1) client generates random code_verifier; (2) client computes code_challenge = BASE64URL(SHA256(code_verifier)); (3) sends code_challenge with authorization request; (4) auth server stores challenge; (5) client sends code_verifier at token exchange; (6) server verifies SHA256(code_verifier) == stored challenge. Attack prevented: authorization code interception — if attacker intercepts the code (via redirect URI open redirect, malicious app on same device), they cannot exchange it without the code_verifier (which was never transmitted).
senior_nuance: PKCE does not replace client_secret in confidential clients — it's additive. OAuth 2.1 requires PKCE for all authorization code flows (even confidential clients). For mobile: use OS-registered custom URL schemes or Universal Links/App Links as redirect URI — these cannot be intercepted by other apps. For SPAs: localhost redirect is only valid in development; production uses the app's origin. PKCE code_verifier must be cryptographically random (min 43 chars, max 128, from unreserved characters) — not predictable.
quote_to_say: PKCE is the difference between 'I got a code' and 'I can prove I initiated the flow' — mandatory for any public client.
